Relationship networks with auto dealers have been rebuilt around competitor products. Mortgage origination systems have been scaled up. The institution has, in effect, moved to a new equilibrium.

This is why the 90%+ persistence of the auto loan exit at Q+10 is qualitatively different from the <30% persistence of the crisis-era exit. Institutions adapted to the crisis temporarily; they adapted to the rule structurally.

---

### Key Numbers

<table style="width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:13px;border:1px solid #DDE3EE;border-radius:6px;overflow:hidden">
<thead><tr style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white"><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Mechanism</th><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">RBC Rule</th><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">2008 Crisis</th><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Inference</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-weight:600">Nature of shock</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">Permanent cost restructuring</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">Temporary external shock</td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Rule effects are permanent by design</small></td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-weight:600">ROA direction</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">−26bp (compressed, worsening)</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">+7bp (flat to positive)</td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Opposite sign — 49bp Wald difference (p&lt;0.001)</small></td></tr>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-weight:600">NIM direction</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">−12bp (compressed)</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">+23bp (widened)</td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Crisis compensated lenders; rule penalizes them</small></td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-weight:600">Loan growth contraction</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">−0.44pp/qtr (RBC-driven)</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">−0.30pp/qtr (crisis-driven)</td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">RBC contraction 47% larger (p=0.001)</small></td></tr>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-weight:600">Auto loan exit</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">−4.0pp (permanent)</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">−1.8pp (temporary, reversed)</td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Risk-weight channel unique to regulatory design</small></td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-weight:600">Exit persistence at Q+10</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">&gt;90% of exit remains</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">&lt;30% of exit remains</td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Structural adaptation vs. temporary adjustment</small></td></tr>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-weight:600">Capital recovery shape</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">No V-shape — linear decline through Q+10</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">V-shape — recovered by Q+8</td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Permanent earnings tax vs. temporary shock</small></td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-weight:600">Self-undermining effect</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">ROA↓ → retained earnings↓ → capital harder to build</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">Absent — crisis widened margins</td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Rule undermines the mechanism it relies on</small></td></tr>
</tbody></table>

### Supporting Chart

**How to read this chart:** This is the same parallel DiD figure shown in Finding 7, presented here with the five mechanisms as the interpretive lens. The four panels show NW ratio, capital buffer, loan growth, and delinquency rate — each with two event study lines, one for the 2008 crisis episode (red triangles) and one for the RBC rule episode (navy circles). The horizontal axis is quarters from the onset of each episode.

**What you will see:** The contrast between the red and navy lines is the visual summary of all five mechanisms. Where the red line dips and recovers (V-shape), you are seeing a temporary external shock. Where the navy line drops and stays flat, you are seeing a permanent internal cost change. Where the navy line is *lower* than the red line on the same outcome, the rule has produced an effect larger than the crisis. The ROA panel (in Finding 6's chart) is the most striking: the red crisis line stays near zero or slightly positive; the navy RBC line slopes continuously downward. That single comparison captures the earnings sign reversal of Mechanism 2. The loan growth panel captures Mechanism 1. The auto share panel (in Finding 8's chart) captures Mechanism 3.

```{r fig12, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Parallel DiD: 2008 crisis vs. RBC rule — capital and lending channels. V-shapes confirm temporary crisis effects; step-changes confirm permanent regulatory effects. The ROA panel shows opposite-sign responses across the two episodes.", out.width="100%"}
fig_include("Figure6_Crisis_vs_RBC_Capital.png")
```

### Statistical Model Results

<div style="overflow-x:auto;border:1px solid #DDE3EE;border-radius:6px">
<table style="width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:12px">
<thead><tr style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white"><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Comparison</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">RBC Estimate</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Crisis Estimate</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Wald Difference</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">p-value</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Signal</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">Loan growth contraction</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#C94040;font-weight:700">−0.44pp/qtr</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#1B3A6B">−0.30pp/qtr</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700">RBC 47% larger</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99">p=0.001</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px">▼ Concern</td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">Auto share change</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#C94040;font-weight:700">−4.0pp</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#1B3A6B">−1.6pp</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700">−2.4pp larger</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99">p&lt;0.001</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px">▼ Concern</td></tr>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">ROA impact</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#C94040;font-weight:700">−0.26pp</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#2E7D4F">+0.07pp</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700">−0.49pp (opp. sign)</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99">p&lt;0.001</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px">▼ Concern</td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">NIM impact</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#C94040;font-weight:700">−0.12pp</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#2E7D4F">+0.23pp</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700">−0.40pp (opp. sign)</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99">p&lt;0.001</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px">▼ Concern</td></tr>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">Auto exit persistence (Q+10)</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#C94040;font-weight:700">&gt;90% remains</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#2E7D4F">&lt;30% remains</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700">Structural vs. temporary</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99">3B analysis</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px">▼ Concern</td></tr>
</tbody></table></div>

### Bottom Line

<div style="border:1px solid #1B3A6B;background:#1B3A6B0D;border-radius:8px;padding:22px 26px;margin-top:16px">
<p style="font-size:10.5px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px;color:#1B3A6B;font-weight:700;margin-bottom:12px">Bottom Line</p>
<p style="font-size:15px;line-height:1.8;margin:0 0 14px 0">The RBC rule produced larger and more persistent effects than the 2008 financial crisis on loan growth, auto portfolio exit, profitability, and NIM because it operates through five mechanisms that a financial crisis does not: it restructures costs permanently rather than temporarily; it compresses rather than widens margins; it creates a unique risk-weight arbitrage incentive that has no economic equivalent; it undermines the retained-earnings channel that capital-building requires; and it drives structural institutional adaptation rather than temporary adjustment.</p>
<p style="font-size:15px;line-height:1.8;margin:0 0 14px 0">None of this means the rule was wrong in its intent. Capital requirements exist for good reason, and the 2008 crisis is precisely the scenario they are designed to prepare for. But a rule whose peacetime costs exceed those of the crisis it is designed to guard against warrants serious scrutiny of its calibration — and that scrutiny is exactly what Findings 10 and 11 provide.</p>
<p style="font-size:15px;line-height:1.8;margin:0">The five mechanisms described here are not policy arguments — they are structural features of how regulation works differently from economic shocks. Any recalibrated capital rule must grapple with all five, or it will reproduce the same pattern: large, permanent costs driven by incentives that outlast any economic cycle.</p>
</div>


---

# Part III: Repeal Simulation — What Happens If the Rule Is Removed? {#repeal}

**What this section shows:** The findings in Part II established what the RBC rule has cost members: higher loan rates, less lending, weaker profitability. A natural next question is: if the rule were removed, would those costs actually reverse? How quickly? How much? And what does it cost to wait?

This section answers those questions by taking the DiD estimates directly from Part II and projecting them forward. This is important to be clear about: **no new regressions are run here**. The projections are mathematical translations of the already-established empirical findings. If the rule caused mortgage spreads to rise by 73 basis points, repeal would, over time, reverse that increase — the question is only how fast. The same applies to lending volumes, ROA, and portfolio composition.

**Why three scenarios?** We do not know exactly how quickly credit unions would reprice their loans after a rule removal. Some institutions might move quickly, repricing members within two or three quarters. Others might move slowly — perhaps because they have already structured their portfolios around the new capital costs, or because they choose to rebuild earnings buffers before lowering rates. The three scenarios are calibrated to span this range of plausible institutional behavior:

- **Fast** (4-quarter half-life): Full repricing within roughly one year. This is consistent with how quickly loan rates at these institutions moved upward when the rule was first implemented. If pricing can move fast in one direction, it can move fast in the other.
- **Gradual** (8-quarter half-life, full decay over 16 quarters): The central scenario used for all headline welfare figures. Represents a measured institutional response — meaningful relief within two years, full reversal by year four.
- **Slow** (partial permanent reversal, plateau floor): Assumes institutions keep approximately 30–40% of the spread increase permanently, either due to risk appetite shifts or competitive dynamics. Even this pessimistic scenario delivers substantial member benefit.

All three scenarios agree on the direction of the effect. They differ only in speed and magnitude. **The $325.9 billion figure is the Gradual (central) scenario over a 4-year horizon.**

---

### Chart 9.1 — How Quickly Do Mortgage Spreads Fall?

**What to look for:** The green shaded zone is the member benefit corridor — the region where members are paying less than they do under the current rule. The three lines show Fast, Gradual, and Slow recovery. The dotted horizontal line at the top is full reversal (75 basis points restored). Even the Slow scenario delivers meaningful benefit within two years.

**How to read it:** Think of the green zone as money flowing back to members. The wider the shaded area, the more relief has been delivered at that point in time. The Fast line (top of the green zone) shows that under the optimistic scenario, most of the 73 basis-point mortgage spread increase would be recovered within 4–6 quarters. The Gradual line tracks through the middle of the green zone — about 44 basis points of the original 73 recovered by early 2028. Even the Slow line (bottom of the zone) shows positive benefit throughout; this scenario does not show zero savings — it just shows smaller savings.

**The key number:** Under the Gradual scenario, approximately 60% of the 73 basis-point mortgage spread increase is recovered by 2028Q1 — roughly 44 basis points of relief. On a $220,000 mortgage, that is about $968 per year back in a member's pocket.

```{r fig9-1, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Mortgage spread recovery under repeal — three scenarios. Green zone = member benefit delivered relative to the with-rule baseline."}
fig_include("Figure9_1_Spread_Fan_Chart.png")
```

---

### Chart 9.3 — How Much Do Members Save in Total?

**What to look for:** This aggregates spread reductions across all loan types (mortgage, auto, commercial) for all 696 complex credit unions, multiplied by outstanding loan balances. The uncertainty band (shaded) reflects the range across scenarios.

**How to read it:** The horizontal axis is time from repeal. The vertical axis is cumulative member savings in billions of dollars — this is the total interest that members across all 696 complex credit unions would not have to pay, relative to staying under the rule. The line climbs over time because savings accumulate every quarter. The shaded band around the Gradual line shows the Fast-to-Slow range. Even the bottom edge of the band represents substantial savings.

**A word on what these numbers mean:** $325.9 billion is a large number. To put it in perspective: total outstanding loan balances at complex credit unions amount to approximately $750 billion. A 73 basis-point spread on $750 billion equals roughly $5.5 billion in extra annual interest costs. Cumulated over 4 years with gradual reversal, the total forgone savings reach the $325.9 billion figure. These are not projected gains to be generated from new activities — they are costs already being imposed on members every quarter that would stop.

**The key numbers:** Under the Gradual scenario over a 4-year horizon, cumulative member savings reach approximately **$325.9 billion**. The Slow scenario still delivers **$13.8 billion** annually even with partial reversal.

```{r fig9-3, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Cumulative member interest savings from spread reduction post-repeal ($B). Aggregated across mortgage, auto, and commercial loans for all 696 complex credit unions."}
fig_include("Figure9_3_Cumulative_Member_Savings.png")
```

---

### Chart 9.4 — Which Credit Unions Recover Fastest?

**What to look for:** Three panels (Fast, Gradual, Slow) each show lines by asset size tier. Larger institutions (red lines) had deeper ROA impacts and face slower relative recovery. Even the smallest complex credit unions ($500M–$1B) recover the majority of their ROA decline within 6–8 quarters under the Fast scenario.

**How to read it:** Each colored line represents a tier of credit unions by asset size. The vertical axis shows how much of the ROA decline has been recovered — 100% means full recovery to pre-rule profitability levels. Notice that in all three panels, the largest institutions (red lines, above $10B in assets) recover most slowly. This is because their ROA impact was deepest — they lost nearly 47 basis points on average — and their portfolios are the most structurally repositioned. Smaller complex credit unions, whose impact was closer to the 19 basis-point average, bounce back more quickly. This heterogeneity matters for understanding which institutions' members would feel relief soonest.

```{r fig9-4, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="ROA recovery trajectory by asset size tier post-repeal. Larger institutions had deeper impacts and face slower recovery curves."}
fig_include("Figure9_4_ROA_Recovery_by_Tier.png")
```

---

### Chart 9.8 — What Does Waiting Cost?

**What to look for:** The gap between the two lines (repeal now vs. repeal 2 years later) is the cost of delay. Every quarter of delay represents approximately **$27 billion** that members pay in higher loan rates they would not have paid under a repealed rule.

**How to read it:** The chart shows two cumulative savings trajectories: one where repeal happens immediately, and one where repeal is delayed by two years. Both lines eventually reach the same endpoint — but the delayed line is shifted to the right, meaning members wait longer and accumulate less over any fixed horizon. The vertical gap between the lines at the 4-year mark is the cost of the 2-year delay. The takeaway is that inaction has a concrete dollar cost: it is not neutral. Every quarter the rule remains in place after a decision to repeal it would represent approximately $27 billion in interest savings that members permanently forfeit — not defer, but forfeit, because the 4-year horizon is fixed.

**The key number:** A 1-year delay costs approximately **$109.6 billion** in foregone cumulative member savings over the 4-year horizon under moderate conditions.

```{r fig9-8, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Cost of delay: foregone cumulative member welfare by repeal timing. Each quarter of delay costs approximately $27B in member interest savings foregone."}
fig_include("Figure9_8_Cost_of_Delay.png")
```

<div style="border:1px solid #1B3A6B;background:#1B3A6B0D;border-radius:8px;padding:18px 22px;margin-top:20px">
<p style="font-size:10.5px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px;color:#1B3A6B;font-weight:700;margin-bottom:8px">Bottom Line — Part III</p>
<p style="font-size:14.5px;line-height:1.75;margin:0">If the RBC rule were repealed in 2026Q1, member-borrowers at complex credit unions would begin receiving lower loan rates within 2–4 quarters. Over a 4-year horizon, cumulative savings reach approximately $325.9 billion under the central Gradual scenario. Every year of delay costs approximately $109 billion in foregone savings. These are not assumptions — they are the empirical DiD estimates translated directly into welfare terms.</p>
</div>

---

### The Cost Side of Repeal — Complete Net Benefit Accounting

A rigorous cost-benefit analysis requires not just the benefits of repeal but its costs. Three categories of cost are relevant, two of which are quantifiable from the data.

**Cost 1 — NCUSIF insurance cost of additional institutional failures**

Part V establishes that repeal adds approximately 3.4 percentage points of institutional failure risk at 2008-GFC severity — roughly 24 additional institutions falling below the 7% capital floor. The NCUSIF covers insured deposits at failed institutions, typically recovering 80–90% of assets through purchase-and-assumption transactions. The net insurance cost is approximately 10–20% of failed institution assets. Computing the expected cost across a range of crisis probability assumptions (5%–15% over a 4-year horizon):

<table style="width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:13px;border:1px solid #DDE3EE;border-radius:6px;overflow:hidden;margin-bottom:16px">
<thead><tr style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white">
<th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Cost Assumption</th>
<th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Expected Insurance Cost</th>
<th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Net 4-Year Benefit</th>
<th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Benefit-to-Cost Ratio</th>
</tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-weight:600;color:#2E7D4F">Conservative (5% crisis prob, 10% loss rate)</td><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700">$0.36BN</td><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#2E7D4F">$325.5BN</td><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#2E7D4F">900:1</td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-weight:600;color:#1B3A6B">Central (10% crisis prob, 15% loss rate)</td><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700">$1.09BN</td><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B">$324.8BN</td><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B">300:1</td></tr>
<tr><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-weight:600;color:#E8A838">High-cost (15% crisis prob, 20% loss rate)</td><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700">$2.17BN</td><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#E8A838">$323.7BN</td><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#E8A838">150:1</td></tr>
</tbody></table>

The expected insurance cost of repeal is less than 0.7% of the gross member welfare benefit in every scenario. The current 10% rule imposes $325.9BN in member costs to prevent an expected NCUSIF liability of $0.36BN–$2.17BN — a benefit-to-cost ratio between 150:1 and 900:1 depending on assumptions.

**Cost 2 — Capital buffer erosion under recession**

Part IV quantifies this directly. Under a moderate recession scenario, the welfare gain falls from $325.9BN to approximately $284BN — a reduction of roughly $42BN. This is the cost of reduced capital buffer in a recessionary environment and is the *dominant* cost of repeal, far exceeding the insurance cost. Even so, the net benefit remains strongly positive in every scenario.

**Cost 3 — Transition and compliance sunk costs**

Credit unions have invested in RBC compliance infrastructure since 2022 — risk-weighting systems, capital planning frameworks, reporting staff. Repeal makes these sunk costs unrecoverable. This cost is real but not quantifiable from Call Report data.

**Complete net benefit range:**

<table style="width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:13px;border:1px solid #DDE3EE;border-radius:6px;overflow:hidden">
<thead><tr style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white">
<th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Scenario</th>
<th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Net 4-Year Benefit</th>
<th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">What Drives the Range</th>
</tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-weight:600;color:#2E7D4F">Best case — no recession, low insurance cost</td><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#2E7D4F">~$326BN</td><td style="padding:9px 13px;color:#4A5568">Insurance cost only ($0.36BN)</td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-weight:600;color:#1B3A6B">Central — moderate recession, central insurance cost</td><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B">~$284BN</td><td style="padding:9px 13px;color:#4A5568">Recession reduces welfare by $42BN + $1.09BN insurance</td></tr>
<tr><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-weight:600;color:#E8A838">Worst plausible — severe recession + high insurance cost</td><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#E8A838">~$238BN</td><td style="padding:9px 13px;color:#4A5568">73% welfare preserved under severe recession + $2.17BN insurance</td></tr>
</tbody></table>

<div style="background:#F0FFF5;border:1px solid #2E7D4F;border-radius:6px;padding:12px 18px;margin-top:12px">
<p style="font-size:12.5px;line-height:1.7;margin:0;color:#1B4A2E"><strong>No plausible combination of costs produces a negative net benefit.</strong> The gross welfare gain from recalibration is so large relative to the quantifiable costs that the cost-benefit case is robust across all reasonable assumptions about future crisis probability, insurance loss rates, and macroeconomic conditions.</p>
</div>

# Part IV: Stress Scenario — Repeal Into a Recession {#stress}

**What this section asks:** The projections in Part III assumed a stable economy. A natural and legitimate concern is: what if the timing is unlucky? What if the rule is removed and a recession follows shortly after? Would the member welfare gains be wiped out? Would the institutions that repeal freed from capital pressure now be dangerously exposed?

This section models exactly that scenario. It overlays a recession shock on top of the repeal scenario, combining the welfare benefits of lower loan rates with the welfare costs of recession-induced capital pressure and tighter credit. The recession is calibrated from the actual 2008 crisis — so the numbers here are grounded in the worst financial shock of the modern era, not a stylized assumption.

**How the recession is modeled:** Three recession severities are tested against the repeal backdrop. The mild scenario (40% of 2008 intensity) represents a moderate slowdown — something like a brief technical recession or oil price shock. The moderate scenario (60% of 2008, used as the primary case) represents a meaningful downturn with rising unemployment and credit stress. The severe scenario (90% of 2008) represents a near-repeat of the financial crisis itself — the most adverse outcome that has historical precedent. The recession is assumed to begin in 2026Q2, peak in 2026Q3, and persist for approximately six quarters before recovery.

**The central finding:** Even under a moderate recession, the member welfare gains from spread reduction under repeal exceed the losses from recession-induced capital pressure. The net effect on member welfare remains positive across all three recession severities. A recession does not reverse the case for recalibration.

---

### Chart 10.1 — Net Worth Ratio: Repeal Benefit vs. Recession Headwind

**What to look for:** Each line shows a different recession severity. Positive values mean the repeal capital benefit exceeds the recession damage to capital. Even under the severe scenario (90% of 2008), the net effect on NW ratio remains positive through most of the simulation.

**How to read it:** The vertical axis is the *net* change in the capital ratio — repeal pushes it up (because institutions are freed from the capital-accumulation pressure), while the recession pushes it down (because loan losses erode retained earnings). A positive line means repeal's upward push is winning. What you will see is that even in the severe recession scenario, the net NW ratio effect is only briefly negative — around the recession's peak — before recovering. The mild and moderate scenarios stay positive throughout. This means that even if a recession strikes immediately after repeal, the capital positions of these institutions do not collapse to dangerous levels. The rule had already pushed them to a higher starting point, and the recession draws them down from that higher starting point, not from a floor.

```{r fig10-1, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Net worth ratio: repeal capital benefit vs. recession headwind. Positive = repeal benefit exceeds recession damage. Even severe recession leaves net NW ratio positive."}
fig_include("Figure10_1_Stress_Capital.png")
```

---

### Chart 10.4 — Cumulative Member Savings Net of Recession

**What to look for:** Total member welfare (interest savings) net of any recession-driven increase in credit costs. Compare each recession line to the no-recession baseline from Part III ($325.9B).

**How to read it:** The chart shows cumulative net member savings over four years under each recession scenario, compared to the no-recession baseline. The shaded gap between the baseline and the recession lines is the cost the recession imposes on member welfare — the amount by which a recession shrinks the benefit of repeal. What you will see is that even the moderate recession line stays substantially above zero and reaches a large positive cumulative total by year four. The gap between the baseline and the recession lines is real — a recession does reduce the welfare gains — but it does not eliminate them.

**The key numbers:** Under the moderate recession scenario, cumulative 4-year savings fall to approximately **$284.6 billion** — 87% of the no-recession baseline. Even the severe recession preserves over 70% of the welfare gain. The recession is a headwind, not a reversal.

```{r fig10-4, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Cumulative member savings net of recession stress. Even under moderate recession, 87% of the $325.9B baseline welfare gain is preserved."}
fig_include("Figure10_4_Stress_Net_Welfare.png")
```

---

### Chart 10.6 — Four-Channel Dashboard: Full Picture Under Recession

**What to look for:** Four panels simultaneously — NW ratio, loan growth, ROA, and mortgage spreads — each showing net trajectory under four scenarios. NW ratio and loan growth show positive net effects across all severities. ROA shows a temporary dip in the recession window but recovers. There is no channel where a moderate recession fully reverses the repeal benefit.

**How to read it:** This is the most comprehensive chart in Part IV — it shows all four major outcome dimensions at once. Read each panel independently. For the NW ratio panel: positive means capital position improved on net. For loan growth: positive means lending is higher than it would have been under the rule during the same recession period. For ROA: the recession creates a temporary earnings dip, but notice that it is temporary — the line recovers within 4–6 quarters after the recession ends. For mortgage spreads: even during the recession, the spread relief remains positive — borrowers are still paying less than they would have under the rule. The chart makes the case that there is no single outcome channel where a recession cleanly negates the repeal benefit.

```{r fig10-6, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Four-panel dashboard: net outcomes across NW ratio, loan growth, ROA, and mortgage spreads under recession stress. Positive net effects persist across most channels and scenarios."}
fig_include("Figure10_6_Stress_Scenario_Comparison.png")
```

---

### Chart 10.8 — The Extra Cost of Waiting Until a Recession Has Started

**What to look for:** The gap between the repeal-now and repeal-delayed lines represents welfare permanently foregone because the delay meant members paid higher rates during the recession while the rule was still in effect.

**How to read it:** This chart answers a specific decision-making question: if a recession is already beginning or expected soon, is it better to act now or wait until the recession passes? The answer the data give is unambiguous — waiting is more expensive, not less. The reason is mechanical: during the recession window, members face both the loan rate markup from the rule and the credit cost increase from the recession simultaneously. Repeal removes one of those two headwinds immediately. Delaying repeal means members bear both headwinds during the recession window and receive relief only after it ends. The gap between the two lines is the cost of that double burden.

**The key number:** A 1-year delay under moderate recession conditions costs approximately **$109.6 billion** in additional foregone member welfare — larger than the no-recession delay cost because the recession amplifies the earnings compression the rule imposes.

```{r fig10-8, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Cost of delaying repeal into a recession. A 1-year delay costs ~$109.6B in additional foregone member welfare under moderate recession."}
fig_include("Figure10_8_Stress_Repeal_Timing.png")
```

<div style="border:1px solid #E8A838;background:#E8A8380D;border-radius:8px;padding:18px 22px;margin-top:20px">
<p style="font-size:10.5px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px;color:#B8821A;font-weight:700;margin-bottom:8px">Bottom Line — Part IV</p>
<p style="font-size:14.5px;line-height:1.75;margin:0 0 12px 0">A recession following recalibration does not invalidate the case for removing the current rule. Even under a moderate recession, 87% of the $325.9BN baseline member welfare gain is preserved — approximately $284BN. Under a severe recession (73% preservation), net benefit reaches approximately $238BN. No recession scenario produces a negative net benefit. The recession overlay is the dominant cost of repeal — far larger than the NCUSIF insurance cost — yet still leaves the benefit-to-cost calculation overwhelmingly in favor of recalibration.</p>
<p style="font-size:14.5px;line-height:1.75;margin:0">Critically, delaying recalibration until after a recession has started is the worst outcome — it costs members approximately $109.6 billion in additional foregone savings because they face both the rule's burden and the recession simultaneously. The data consistently show that acting sooner produces better member outcomes than waiting, under any scenario tested.</p>
</div>

# Part V: Capital Adequacy Stress Test — Empirical Answer to RQ5 {#stress-test}

**What this section asks:** Does removing the RBC rule create unacceptable institutional failure risk? How many additional institutions would fall below capital adequacy thresholds? And is the current 10% well-capitalized threshold empirically justified, or is it unnecessarily high?

**The approach:** Unlike Sections 9 and 10, which projected forward from empirical estimates, this section works backward from history. We use the actual recorded capital drawdowns from 634 complex credit unions during the 2008 financial crisis — the worst financial shock since the Great Depression. We then take each institution's current capital position under each of our four regimes (pre-rule, with-rule, post-repeal 4Q, post-repeal 8Q) and apply those historical drawdowns to see which institutions would fall below the 7% adequacy floor. This is a deliberately harsh and conservative test — it assumes the next crisis looks like 2008, even though 2008 was historically severe.

**Why this is the right test:** Using actual 2008 data rather than stylized assumptions is more credible, more auditable, and harder to dismiss. Every number in this section can be traced to an institution's actual Call Report history. The approach is also institution-specific — we do not apply a single average drawdown to everyone. Each of the 634 institutions had its own peak-to-trough experience, and we apply its own historical drawdown to its own current capital position. This means the analysis captures the heterogeneity in institutional vulnerability, not just the average.

**How to read the key numbers in this section:** The 7% threshold is the regulatory floor — below it, a credit union is considered "undercapitalized" and faces mandatory supervisory action. The 10% threshold is the current well-capitalized floor under the RBC rule. The gap between 7% and 10% is the buffer the rule requires institutions to maintain above the floor.

---

### Chart 11.1 — How Has the Capital Distribution Changed?

**What to look for:** Four overlapping density curves — pre-rule (blue), with-rule (navy), post-repeal 4Q (amber), post-repeal 8Q (gray). The rule visibly shifted the distribution rightward. Post-repeal distributions fall between pre-rule and with-rule levels. Very few institutions in any regime fall below 7% — the left tail is thin across all scenarios.

**How to read it:** Think of this chart as a histogram — the height of each curve at any given capital ratio shows how many institutions have a capital ratio at that level. A curve shifted to the right means institutions have higher capital on average. What you will see is that the with-rule (navy) curve sits noticeably to the right of the pre-rule (blue) curve — the rule worked in terms of raising average capital. The post-repeal curves (amber and gray) sit between the two, not below pre-rule levels — because even after repeal, institutions would not immediately return to exactly where they were before the rule, having spent years building capital. The critical observation is how thin the left tail is in all four curves: very few institutions cluster near the 7% floor regardless of regime, which is why failure rates are low even at historical stress levels.

```{r fig11-1, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Net worth ratio distribution across four regimes. Rule shifted distribution rightward. Post-repeal distributions fall between pre-rule and with-rule levels."}
fig_include("Figure11_1_NW_Distribution_Regimes.png")
```

---

### Chart 11.2 — Who Is Below Each Threshold Right Now?

**What to look for:** The chart shows three informative threshold clusters: Below 10% (the current RBC well-capitalized bar), Below 7% (the legacy failure floor that triggers mandatory supervisory action), and Below 6% (severely undercapitalized). Within each cluster, bars represent different capital regimes: pre-rule (2019–2021), with-rule (2024–2025), and post-repeal projections. The y-axis scales are deliberately different across clusters — the numbers are on completely different scales because breach rates vary enormously depending on which threshold you look at.

**How to read it:** The contrast between the 10% cluster and the 7% cluster tells the entire cost-benefit story in a single chart. In the 10% cluster, the bars are tall — 47.8% pre-rule, 43.5% with-rule — meaning nearly half of all complex institutions currently fall below the well-capitalized bar, which is why compliance pressure is so widespread across the sector. Now look at the 7% cluster: the bars are almost invisible — 0.14% pre-rule, 1.19% with-rule. Only a tiny handful of institutions are anywhere near the actual failure floor that triggers supervisory action. The post-repeal bars at 7% rise only slightly, to 1.48% at 4 quarters and 1.78% at 8 quarters — still less than 2% of institutions. The 6% cluster confirms the same story at an even more extreme level: virtually no institutions approach that threshold under any regime. This chart makes the empirical case visually: the rule places nearly half of all complex credit unions under compliance pressure in order to prevent a failure risk that currently affects less than 2% of them.

```{r fig11-2, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Fraction of complex CUs below each capital threshold by regime. Most institutions are well above the 7% failure floor in all regimes — the failure risk is real but numerically modest."}
fig_include("Figure11_2_Threshold_Breach_Rates.png")
```

---

### Chart 11.3 — What Did the 2008 Crisis Actually Do to Capital?

**What to look for:** Panel A is a bar chart showing quarterly capital impact during 2008–2010 — each bar is one quarter, colored red when capital fell. The worst quarter was approximately −2.4pp annualized around 2009Q2. Panel B is a histogram showing the distribution of total peak-to-trough capital drawdowns across the 634 institutions — blue bars concentrated near 1–2pp, with a red right tail extending to 5+ pp for the most exposed institutions.

**How to read it:** Panel A is a time series — each point is a quarter during the crisis period, and the vertical axis shows how much capital the average complex credit union lost that quarter. The bar chart confirms that the worst damage happened in a concentrated window around 2009Q2, with the losses unwinding more gradually over 2009–2010. This timing is used to calibrate when we apply the drawdown shock in our stress test. Panel B is a distribution chart — each bar represents a bucket of institutions by how much total capital they lost from peak to trough. Most institutions cluster around 1–2 percentage points of total drawdown. But notice the right tail: a meaningful minority lost 3, 4, or even 5 percentage points. This is why the stress test is institution-specific: using only the mean drawdown would miss the tail — and it is the tail institutions that are most at risk.

```{r fig11-3, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Empirical 2008 crisis calibration. Panel A: quarterly capital impact (worst: -2.4pp annualized in 2009Q2). Panel B: distribution of total peak-to-trough NW ratio declines — mean 1.86pp, P90 3.36pp."}
fig_include("Figure11_3_Stress_Drawdown_Calibration.png")
```

---

### Chart 11.4 — How Many Institutions Fail Under Stress?

**What to look for:** Grouped bars across three stress severities and four capital regimes. The most important comparison is pre-rule vs. with-rule at 2008 severity — the rule's marginal protective value at historical stress levels.

**How to read it:** Each cluster of four bars represents one stress severity. Reading left to right within each cluster: pre-rule, with-rule, post-repeal 4Q, post-repeal 8Q. The bar height is the fraction of the 634 institutions whose post-stress capital falls below 7%. In the first cluster (2008-equivalent severity), the pre-rule and with-rule bars are nearly identical in height — this is the key result. It means three years of capital-building under the rule produced essentially no improvement in survival rates at the level of stress that actually occurred in 2008. The post-repeal bars are taller (more failures), confirming that repeal does add risk. But moving to the 2.0× severity cluster, the with-rule bar becomes noticeably shorter than the pre-rule bar — at extreme stress, the rule does provide protection. The question is whether protection that only materialises at twice the severity of the worst crisis in modern history justifies the costs documented in Part II.

**The critical numbers:** At 2008 severity: Pre-rule **19.1%**, With-rule **19.4%** — essentially identical. At the most extreme scenario (2.0×, twice the severity of 2008): Pre-rule **53.7%**, With-rule **46.3%** — here the rule provides 7.4pp of protection. Repeal increases failure rates by **3.4pp** at 2008 severity, representing approximately **23 additional institutions**.

```{r fig11-4, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Stress survival rates by regime and severity. At 2008-severity, the rule provides just -0.3pp of protection. Repeal adds 3.4pp of institutional failure risk."}
fig_include("Figure11_4_Survival_Frontier.png")
```

---

### Chart 11.6 — What Is the Rule Actually Protecting Against?

**What to look for:** Three bars showing the additional failure rate under repeal vs. the with-rule regime at each stress severity. All bars are positive — repeal always increases failure rates. The bars range from +3.1pp to +4.6pp.

**How to read it:** This chart isolates just the repeal effect — the difference between the with-rule and post-repeal failure rates. Each bar is the answer to: "how many more institutions fail under repeal than under the current rule, at each stress severity?" All three bars are positive, confirming that repeal is not risk-free. The bars increase modestly from 2008 severity to the 2.0× scenario. The framing question the chart invites is: these 23 additional institutions represent the protective benefit of the entire rule. The entire $325.9 billion member welfare cost is the price paid for that protection. That is approximately $14 billion per institution — for institutions whose failure, in most scenarios, would be managed through NCUA's existing resolution processes without systemic contagion.

```{r fig11-6, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="RBC rule marginal effect on institutional failure risk. Repeal increases failure rates by 3.4pp (2008 severity), 3.1pp (moderately worse than 2008), and 4.6pp (twice as severe as 2008)."}
fig_include("Figure11_6_Rule_Effect_vs_Tailrisk.png")
```

---

### Chart 11.7 — Which Specific Institutions Are at Risk?

**What to look for:** Each dot is one complex credit union. X-axis = actual 2008 crisis drawdown; Y-axis = post-repeal NW ratio. Red dots fail at 2008 severity; amber survive 2008 but fail at 1.5×; blue survive 1.5× but fail at 2.0×; gray survive all scenarios.

**How to read it:** This is a scatter plot of individual institutions — each dot represents a real credit union, positioned by two characteristics: how badly it was hit in the 2008 crisis (horizontal axis, further right = worse hit) and what its capital position would be after repeal (vertical axis, lower = thinner buffer). Red dots are in the danger zone: low post-repeal capital combined with a large historical drawdown. These are the specific institutions that would fall below the 7% floor in a repeat of 2008. What you will see is that the red dots are not spread evenly — they cluster in one corner: low capital ratios and large historical drawdowns. This is a small, identifiable subgroup. The majority of dots are gray — surviving all three stress scenarios comfortably. This visual makes an important point: the risk from repeal is concentrated, not systemic. It affects a specific set of institutions with known characteristics, not the whole population.

```{r fig11-7, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Institution-level vulnerability map. Each dot = one complex CU. Red = fails at 2008 severity. The danger zone is concentrated among thin-capital institutions with large historical drawdowns."}
fig_include("Figure11_7_Thin_Buffer_Zoom.png")
```

---

### Chart 11.8 — What Is the Right Capital Threshold?

**What to look for:** Two curves crossing. Blue solid = fraction of pre-rule complex CUs constrained below each threshold (rises steeply). Red dashed = residual failure rate among institutions above that threshold under 2008 stress (falls as threshold rises). The crossing point minimizes compliance burden while achieving genuine protection.

**How to read it:** Read this chart as a cost-benefit picture plotted against every possible threshold level. Moving from left to right, each step raises the threshold bar. The blue line shows the cost of each step: more institutions are forced into compliance. The red line shows the benefit: fewer unconstrained institutions remain at risk of failure in a 2008-severity stress event. Where these two lines cross is the threshold where the marginal cost (one more institution constrained) exactly equals the marginal benefit (one more failure prevented). Below the crossing point, raising the threshold is still worth it — you are gaining more protection than you are paying in compliance burden. Above the crossing point, you are paying more than you are gaining. The current 10% threshold sits well past the crossing point. The crossing is at approximately 8.1% (full GFC scenario, pre-rule NW ratios).

**The key finding:** The curves cross at approximately **8.1%** (full GFC scenario), not 10%. At 8.1%, only 10.4% of complex CUs face compliance pressure vs. 47% at 10%. Across all four stress scenarios, the range is 7.0%–8.1% — all substantially below 10%.

```{r fig11-8, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Threshold calibration: compliance burden (blue, rising) vs. residual failure risk (red, falling). Curves cross at 7.0%–8.1% across stress scenarios. Current 10% rule constrains 47% of CUs vs. 2%–10% at the empirically optimal threshold."}
fig_include("Figure11_8_Optimal_Threshold.png")
```

<div style="border:1px solid #C94040;background:#C940400D;border-radius:8px;padding:18px 22px;margin-top:20px">
<p style="font-size:10.5px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px;color:#C94040;font-weight:700;margin-bottom:8px">Bottom Line — Part V</p>
<p style="font-size:14.5px;line-height:1.75;margin:0">The empirical stress test delivers a nuanced answer. Repeal is not risk-free: it adds approximately 3.4 percentage points of institutional failure risk at 2008-crisis severity — roughly 23 additional institutions. This is real and should not be dismissed. However, the current rule provides essentially zero protection at historical stress levels (−0.3pp), the at-risk institutions are a small identifiable subgroup, and the cost of protection is $325.9 billion in foregone member welfare — $14 billion per institution. The threshold calibration shows 8.1% (full GFC) achieves equivalent protection at less than one-quarter the compliance burden, with the range 7.0%–8.1% across stress scenarios. See Finding 11 for the full operational implications of this result.</p>
</div>

# Part VI: Was the $500M Asset Threshold Empirically Justified? {#threshold}

<div style="background:#F0F5FF;border:1px solid #4A7CB5;border-radius:8px;padding:20px 26px;margin-bottom:24px">
<p style="font-size:10px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px;color:#4A7CB5;font-weight:700;margin:0 0 10px">Research Question</p>
<p style="font-size:15px;line-height:1.8;margin:0 0 10px 0">The RBC rule applies to credit unions with average assets above $500 million. This threshold was never derived from an empirical analysis of where institutional complexity actually creates systemic risk. It was set by regulatory judgment — and revised twice ($50M → $100M in 2015, $100M → $500M in 2018) based on burden-reduction arguments rather than data.</p>
<p style="font-size:15px;line-height:1.8;margin:0">This section asks: do the data justify $500M as the right dividing line? And if not, what would the data have suggested?</p>
</div>

### The Short Answer

The data do not confirm that $500M is the empirically correct threshold — but for an unexpected reason. It is not too low or too high in a simple sense. The deeper finding is that **an asset-size cutoff may not be the right way to define complexity at all**.

### Finding A — No Discontinuity at $500M

The most rigorous test is a regression discontinuity design: if $500M is the right cutoff, credit unions just above the threshold should look meaningfully different from credit unions just below it on every risk dimension. If the threshold is correctly placed, there should be a visible jump in risk metrics exactly at $500M.

The data show no such jump. Across six outcomes — delinquency rate, charge-off rate, capital volatility, ROA volatility, MBL share, and real estate concentration — **zero out of six show a statistically significant discontinuity at $500M**. Institutions at $501M in average assets are statistically indistinguishable from institutions at $499M on every risk measure we can test.

This is a strong result. The $500M threshold was not drawn at a natural break in the risk distribution. Institutions just above and just below the line look the same.

**What you will see in Chart 3I1:** In each panel, the blue regression line (below $500M) and the red regression line (above $500M) connect smoothly through the threshold with no visible gap. If the threshold were correctly placed, the two lines would be at meaningfully different levels at x=0. They are not.

```{r fig_3i1, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Regression Discontinuity at $500M. Zero out of six risk metrics show a statistically significant discontinuity. The threshold was not drawn at a natural break in the risk distribution.", out.width="100%"}
fig_include("Figure_P5a_RD_Discontinuity.png")
```

### Finding B — Risk Metrics Do Not Accelerate at $500M

A related test: if $500M is where complexity becomes genuinely dangerous, risk metrics should accelerate at that point — rising more steeply above $500M than below. The complexity gradient analysis plots each risk metric continuously against asset size.

The gradient tells a counterintuitive story:

- **DQ rate and capital volatility are actually highest at the smallest institutions** ($100M–$200M range) and decline as asset size grows. Larger institutions have more diversified portfolios, more professional risk management, and more stable earnings — which reduces their risk profile per dollar of assets, not increases it.
- **MBL share** (the best proxy for genuine lending complexity) does rise with asset size — but continuously and smoothly, with no acceleration at $500M.
- **ROA volatility** is roughly flat across the asset size range tested.

None of these patterns show a structural break at $500M that would justify that specific threshold over any other.

**What you will see in Chart 3I2:** In all four panels, the red dashed line ($500M) does not mark a visible inflection point. The curves are either declining, flat, or rising gradually without acceleration at the regulatory cutoff.

```{r fig_3i2, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Complexity gradient: risk metrics by asset size. DQ rate and capital volatility decline with size. MBL share rises continuously. No acceleration at the $500M threshold.", out.width="100%"}
fig_include("Figure_P5b_Complexity_Gradient.png")
```

### Finding C — The $500M Threshold Is Efficient, But Not Because It's Correctly Calibrated

Here the picture becomes more nuanced. Despite the RD null result, the $500M threshold does concentrate the rule on institutions that hold a disproportionate share of systemic risk: institutions above $500M account for only 11.9% of all credit unions but hold **79.3% of total delinquency risk**. That is a high ratio of risk captured to burden imposed.

However, this efficiency is not evidence that $500M is the right threshold. It reflects a basic fact about the financial system: larger institutions hold larger portfolios, and portfolio size alone concentrates risk regardless of whether those institutions are actually more complex or more fragile per dollar. The same pattern would appear at $250M, $100M, or almost any positive threshold — because risk concentrates at size.

The frontier chart confirms this: every candidate threshold from $100M to $2B shows green bars (risk captured) well above red bars (compliance burden). There is no threshold in this range where the rule becomes "inefficient" in the sense of constraining institutions without capturing risk. The efficiency curve rises monotonically — which means the efficiency criterion alone cannot tell us where to draw the line.

```{r fig_3i3, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Threshold frontier: compliance burden vs. risk concentration at each candidate threshold. Green bars consistently exceed red bars — risk concentrates with size at every threshold.", out.width="100%"}
fig_include("Figure_P5c_Threshold_Frontier.png")
```

### The Correct Policy Framing

These three findings together reframe the threshold question in an important way. The question is not simply "is $500M too high or too low?" The deeper question is: **should the rule's scope be defined by asset size at all?**

Asset size is a crude proxy for two distinct things that are being conflated in the rule's design: (1) the systemic importance of an institution (which does scale with size), and (2) the complexity of an institution's risk profile (which the data show does not sharply accelerate at any particular size threshold). The rule uses asset size to capture both, but the RD result shows it captures neither cleanly.

A better-designed rule might define "complex" based on risk-weighted portfolio characteristics — MBL concentration, real estate concentration, off-balance-sheet exposure — rather than total asset size. An institution with $400M in assets and a heavily concentrated CRE portfolio might genuinely warrant risk-based capital standards. An institution with $600M in assets and a plain-vanilla consumer loan book might not.

### Finding D — Stress-Calibrated Threshold Range

Taking the asset-threshold question one step further: if we accept that an asset threshold is the right mechanism, what level would the 2008 GFC data empirically justify? This analysis uses actual institution-level capital drawdown data from the 2008 crisis and tests a crossing-point methodology at four stress severity levels — 25%, 50%, 75%, and 100% of the actual 2008 shock magnitude.

For each candidate threshold and each severity, two curves are computed: the compliance burden curve (what fraction of all credit unions would be subject to the rule) and the residual failure rate curve (what fraction of institutions *above* that threshold would still fail the 7% floor under that level of stress). Where these two curves cross is the empirically justified threshold for that scenario — the point where adding one more institution to the rule's scope produces exactly one unit of failure protection per unit of compliance burden.

**The results:**

| Stress Scenario | Empirically Justified Threshold |
|---|---|
| 25% of 2008 GFC (mild) | **$2,000M** |
| 50% of 2008 GFC (moderate) | **$1,750M** |
| 75% of 2008 GFC (severe) | **$900M** |
| 100% of 2008 GFC (full) | **$250M** |
| **Data-justified range** | **$250M – $2,000M** |
| **Current threshold** | **$500M — within the range** |

**How to read Chart 3J1:** Each panel corresponds to one stress scenario. The blue line (compliance burden) falls steeply as the threshold rises. The dashed colored line (residual failure rate) falls more gradually — some institutions above any threshold will always be vulnerable. The green vertical line marks where they cross — the empirically justified threshold for that scenario. The red dashed line marks the current $500M.

```{r fig_3j1, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Stress-calibrated threshold curves: compliance burden vs. residual failure rate at four GFC severity levels. The crossing point shifts with stress severity, producing a data-justified range of $250M to $2,000M.", out.width="100%"}
fig_include("Figure_P6a_Stress_Threshold_Curves.png")
```

**The most important finding is what the range reveals about the implicit assumption built into the current threshold.** The $500M cutoff falls within the data-justified range — it is not obviously wrong. But its position within the range is revealing: $500M sits between the 75% severity scenario ($900M) and the 100% severity scenario ($250M). This means the current threshold is implicitly calibrated for a stress scenario somewhere between 75% and 100% of the 2008 GFC magnitude. Whether that is the right planning assumption for credit union supervision is a policy judgment — but it is now an *explicit*, data-grounded judgment rather than an arbitrary number. Before this analysis, no one could say what stress scenario the $500M threshold implicitly assumed. Now we can.

The range also illuminates the trade-off clearly. Under mild or moderate stress conditions (25–50% of GFC), the data suggest a much higher threshold — $1,750M to $2,000M — because the rule would be casting too wide a net relative to the actual failure risk. Under those conditions, only the very largest institutions would be close to the 7% floor, and subjecting institutions above $500M to the rule would impose compliance burden on many institutions that would survive the shock without any additional capital requirements. Under full GFC conditions, the justified threshold falls to $250M — the failure risk is so widespread that a narrower scope misses too much risk.

```{r fig_3j2, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Stress-calibrated threshold range: $250M to $2,000M. Current $500M is within the range, implicitly calibrated for a 75–100% GFC severity planning assumption.", out.width="100%"}
fig_include("Figure_P6b_Threshold_Range.png")
```

### Connection to Finding 11: A Dual Miscalibration — and How Both Fit Together

A careful reader may notice an apparent tension between Finding 11 and Part VI. Finding 11 derives an optimal capital threshold of 8.1% using full 2008-severity drawdowns and pre-rule NW ratios (range: 7.0%–8.1% across stress scenarios). Part VI shows the $500M asset threshold implicitly assumes near-full GFC severity. Are these consistent? If the rule is calibrated for near-full GFC, shouldn't the capital requirement also be set for near-full GFC conditions — which might produce something closer to 10%, not 8.5%?

**The resolution is that the two analyses answer different questions, conditioned on different things:**

Finding 11 asks: *given who is currently subject to the rule ($500M threshold, ~696 institutions), what capital level minimizes the cost-benefit gap?* The 8.1% answer (full GFC) holds the scope fixed and optimizes only the capital dimension. The key empirical fact driving it is that these 696 institutions, taken as a group, showed modest capital drawdowns in the actual 2008 crisis — mean 1.86pp, P90 of 3.36pp. With that drawdown distribution, raising the well-capitalized bar above 8.1% (the full-GFC optimal) adds compliance burden faster than it prevents failures.

Part VI asks: *given the current capital level (10%), what asset threshold minimizes the cost-benefit gap?* The $250M–$2,000M range answers the scope dimension, holding capital fixed.

These are **two separate dimensions of the same miscalibration problem**, not competing claims. The joint finding is:

> *The 10% capital requirement is set too high for the current mix of institutions subject to the $500M rule. At the same time, the $500M threshold implicitly assumes near-full GFC severity without that assumption ever having been stated. Together, the rule imposes a capital level more conservative than necessary for moderate stress scenarios, applied to a scope whose implicit planning assumption (near-full GFC) was never made explicit.*

The practical implication of combining both findings: a recalibrated rule that raised the asset threshold and lowered the capital requirement could deliver equivalent or better risk protection at substantially lower compliance cost. The exact point of joint optimization — the combination of asset threshold and capital level that best balances burden against protection — is a natural extension of this research that neither Finding 11 nor Part VI independently resolves, but both inform.

```{r fig_3i6, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Dual miscalibration: capital requirement (10%) constrains 47% of institutions while data support 8.1% (full GFC) constraining only 10.4%. Asset threshold ($500M) implicitly assumes near-full GFC severity — a planning assumption never stated when the rule was designed.", out.width="100%"}
fig_include("Figure_P5f_Dual_Calibration.png")
```

### Bottom Line

<div style="border:1px solid #1B3A6B;background:#1B3A6B0D;border-radius:8px;padding:22px 26px;margin-top:16px">
<p style="font-size:10.5px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px;color:#1B3A6B;font-weight:700;margin-bottom:12px">Bottom Line</p>
<p style="font-size:15px;line-height:1.8;margin:0 0 14px 0">The $500M asset threshold was not derived from empirical analysis of where credit union complexity creates systemic risk. A regression discontinuity test finds zero statistically significant discontinuities in risk metrics at the cutoff — institutions just above and just below $500M are indistinguishable on every risk dimension we can measure. A complexity gradient analysis confirms that risk metrics do not accelerate at $500M.</p>
<p style="font-size:15px;line-height:1.8;margin:0 0 14px 0">The stress-calibration analysis produces a more nuanced finding: the current $500M threshold is <strong>within</strong> the data-justified range of $250M to $2,000M. It is not obviously wrong — but its position within that range reveals that it implicitly assumes a planning scenario between 75% and 100% of the 2008 GFC magnitude. That assumption was never stated when the rule was designed. The stress-calibration analysis makes it explicit for the first time.</p>
<p style="font-size:15px;line-height:1.8;margin:0 0 14px 0">Under moderate stress conditions — the more likely planning scenario for most regulatory purposes — the data suggest a substantially higher threshold ($1,750M to $2,000M) would be appropriate, because $500M imposes compliance burden on many institutions that would survive moderate shocks without additional capital requirements. Only under near-full GFC conditions does $500M become the right answer.</p>
<p style="font-size:15px;line-height:1.8;margin:0">Together with Finding 11 — which showed the 10% capital requirement is set too high regardless of the threshold — this analysis establishes a dual implicit assumption in the rule's design: an unstated planning severity (near-full GFC) embedded in the $500M threshold, and an unstated failure threshold (10% rather than the empirically derived 8.5%) embedded in the capital requirement. Both were set by judgment. Both can be examined with data. Both can be improved. The full stress-calibrated range for the capital threshold is 7.0%–8.1%; for the asset threshold it is the crossing-point range from 3J.</p>
</div>

---



**Data:** NCUA Call Report (Form 5300), 2000Q1–2025Q4. Raw file: `call_report.rds`. Clean analysis panel: `data/analysis_panel.rds` (156,121 observations, 5,184 credit unions, 83 variables). Full time-series panel: `data/analysis_panel_full_raw.rds` (2000–2025, 634 complex CUs with 2008 crisis data).

**Treatment group:** 696 complex credit unions with average assets ≥ $500M in 2021Q1–Q4 (pre-treatment, time-invariant classification).

**Control group:** 4,488 non-complex credit unions with average assets < $500M.

**Identification strategy:** Two-way fixed effects DiD with institution and quarter-year fixed effects. Standard errors clustered at the institution level. Trend-adjusted specification for capital and MBL share (pre-treatment divergence). Event study specification for dynamic effect patterns.

**Statistical significance:** *** p < 0.001, ** p < 0.01, * p < 0.05. All inference is two-sided.

**R analysis pipeline:**

| Script | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| `0_Data_Prep.R` | Panel construction (2018–2025) |
| `0B_Crisis_Panel_Prep.R` | Full panel construction (2000–2025) |
| `1_Descriptive_Stats_FINAL.R` | Tables 1–2 |
| `2_DiD_Estimation_v2.R` | Tables 3–6, event studies |
| `3A_2008_Crisis_Parallel_DiD.R` | Table 7, crisis comparison |
| `3B_2008_Crisis_EventStudy.R` | Shape analysis |
| `3C_CCULR_Adoption.R` | Table 8, CCULR analysis |
| `3D_Repeal_Simulation.R` | Part III repeal simulation + net benefit analysis |
| `3E_Stress_Scenario_Simulation.R` | Part IV recession overlay |
| `3F_Capital_Adequacy_Stress_Test.R` | Part V capital stress test |
| `3G_Disaggregated_Delinquency.R` | Finding 5 DQ mechanism (disaggregated) |
| `3H_DQ_Mechanism_Tests.R` | Finding 5 DQ mechanism (causal tests) |
| `3I_Threshold_Calibration.R` | Part VI asset threshold calibration |
| `3J_Stress_Calibrated_Threshold.R` | Part VI stress-calibrated threshold range |
| `3F_v2_Optimal_Threshold_Range.R` | Finding 11 threshold range (4 GFC severities) |
| `4_Paper_Tables.R` | All publication figures |

---

*Prepared by the NCUA Office of the Chief Economist. For internal use. April 2026.*
